per_question.response: Lars von Trier advises against juggling multiple projects simultaneously. When Sean Baker asked him if he creatively juggles projects, von Trier replied, "No, I put one film entirely to bed before moving on to my next," adding that he only does one at a time and does not focus on anything else [source 1].
per_question.reference: He advises against 'creatively juggling' — he puts one film entirely to bed before moving on to the next, focusing on only one project at a time.

per_question.user_input: How did Sean Baker resolve feeling torn between developing two projects at once?
retrieved_contexts:
because, you know, I
started making my own films when I was about 15, 16 years old. Music videos, short films,
you know, whatever it was. And I realized when I was, you know, setting up Childhood of a Leader, which I didn't end up
making until I was 24, that the only way I would get that movie done is if I was
only focused on that one thing. Because I now know well, that this is not a job that
you can sort of do part-time. - 100%. And I can't creatively juggle. And-
- Me either, me either. - And I actually was really struggling right after Florida Project, 'cause I was starting to get, you know, this attention from studios or
producers who wanted to work and say, "Oh, let's develop this series as you're prepping for your next film." You know? And I was starting
to get seduced in those areas, and I was actually feeling very torn, because I couldn't, I
literally could not focus on one project while trying to
develop, and I just couldn't.

- Yeah.
- I got back to LA, and there was a show that
was literally green lit. And I said, "Sorry
guys, I'm not doing it." And I went ahead into the
development of my next feature, which I was in development for two years before COVID killed it. But the reason I bring
that up is 'cause yeah, von Trier really helped me, really helped me get to a place where- - Well, I mean, there is this,
you know, sort of like myopia and obsession, you know, that I... For or for worse, it seems to
be a requirement of the job. Making a movie is a marriage,
it's not a one night stand. And it is like, you really... It's also, it's baggage you carry with you for your entire life. - [Sean] Yeah. - And so you wanna make
sure that, you know, you're able at least,
you know, for yourself to be able to defend,
you know, why you made that decision at that time. - Right.
- And try to choose

So we had about 10 days in each world, or sometimes five days in any given world. And so we would go to that
location each weekend, whatever that location was going to be, and work through the big scenes together. And so that made it so
that I then had a few days to come up with a way to shoot that scene. And there were some scenes
where his action is so clear that he doesn't really have any choices. And those I could map out in advance, like him going downstairs and running across the
street to get the water and then running back. All of that was mapped
out and storyboarded. I mean, in some ways, a big thing I learned on
"Hereditary" and "Midsommar" was I didn't do any coverage there. I mean, I don't get coverage. I hate coverage, but
everything is sequenced. And so many scenes in those
first two films were oners
per_question.response:
Sean Baker resolved the feeling of being torn by realizing that he cannot "creatively juggle" multiple projects [source 1]. He found that he must be "only focused on that one thing" to get a movie done, noting that filmmaking is not a job that can be done part-time [source 1]. 

When faced with pressure from studios to develop a series while prepping for his next film, he ultimately chose to decline the other work, stating, "Sorry guys, I'm not doing it," so he could focus entirely on the development of his next feature [source 1, source 2].
per_question.reference: He adopted Lars von Trier's rule of not creatively juggling: focusing on one film completely and finishing it before moving on to the next.

and figure out the how. And eventually you gain
fluency, as I'm sure you have. But so the clarity of that,
and the kind of critical need to be decisive as a
leader, as a filmmaker, that I learned early,

to the director to create that portal for them so that they know who the character is and and all that and it's it's always especially hard when you're in a fantastic environment that they've never been in i get a feeling that you surrounded them with sets and with costumes that gave them all that to to work with are you a director that rears a lot with your actors do you prefer the spontaneity i like preparation and spontaneity i like to read through and then we'll just jump up out of the read-through and we'll rehearse i'd like to try to find like the key moment of a scene with the actors way in advance so i can always protect it cinematically later so i know where we're going but then on the day of i really like to give them a lot of freedom to explore and try different things and go up go down turn their backs on each other whatever it is i don't like to usually come in with a strong blocking i may have it in the back of my mind and i may suddenly push them toward a blocking
per_question.response:
For Matt Reeves, the "blank page" or "blank screen" is one of the scariest parts of the filmmaking process [source 1]. To overcome this, he finds it helpful to write specific shots into the script [source 1, 2]. 

He describes this as a way to create a "handle"—a concrete idea of where the camera might go—which provides a starting point for his work [source 1]. Even if the camera does not ultimately end up in that specific position, having the shot written down serves as a "way in" to the scene [source 1].
per_question.reference: He writes shot ideas directly into the script as a 'handle' for where the camera will go, treating the creative process like feeling around a dark room until something connects.
